title: Forms and User Input
description: In this lesson, you will learn how websites collect information from users. You will build forms similar to login pages, registration pages, and contact forms. This is one of the most important skills in web development because almost every website uses forms.
learningObjectives: Understand what HTML forms are and how they work
Create input fields for different types of data
Use labels to make forms easy to understand

subtopics.title: What is a Form?
subtopics.content:
A form is used to collect information from users.

Examples in real life:
- Login form
- Registration form
- Contact form

Basic structure:
```html
<form action="/submit" method="POST">
</form>
```

Explanation:
- action = where data is sent
- method = how data is sent (GET or POST)

Beginner tip:
HTML forms collect data, but they need a backend to process it.
commonMisconceptions: Forms do not store data by themselves.
GET is not safe for passwords.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: A form is like a paper you fill and submit at an office.

subtopics.title: Input Fields (Collecting Data)
subtopics.content:
Input fields allow users to enter information.

Examples:
```html
<input type="text" placeholder="Enter your name" />
<input type="email" placeholder="Enter your email" />
<input type="password" />
<input type="number" />
<input type="date" />
```

Each type helps the browser understand what kind of data is expected.

Beginner tip:
Use the correct input type to improve user experience.
commonMisconceptions: All input fields are not the same — type matters.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Input fields are like different boxes on a form.

subtopics.title: Labels, Textarea, and Dropdowns
subtopics.content:
Labels describe input fields.

Example:
```html
<label for="name">Name:</label>
<input id="name" type="text" />
```

Textarea (for long text):
```html
<textarea rows="4" cols="30"></textarea>
```

Dropdown menu:
```html
<select>
  <option>Male</option>
  <option>Female</option>
</select>
```

Beginner tip:
Always use labels — they make your form easier to use.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Labels are like instructions telling users what to fill.

subtopics.title: Buttons (Submitting Forms)
subtopics.content:
Buttons are used to submit the form.

Example:
```html
<button type="submit">Submit</button>
```

Alternative:
```html
<input type="submit" value="Submit" />
```

Beginner tip:
Always specify the button type.
commonMisconceptions: Buttons without type may behave incorrectly.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Buttons are like the 'Submit' button on a registration form.

subtopics.title: HTML5 Validation (Preventing Errors)
subtopics.content:
Validation helps ensure users enter correct data.

Example:
```html
<input type="email" required />
<input type="text" pattern="[A-Za-z]+" />
```

Common attributes:
- required
- placeholder
- pattern

Beginner tip:
Validation improves data quality but does not replace backend checks.
commonMisconceptions: HTML validation is not fully secure.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Validation is like checking a form before submitting it.
